key_points: Sound is produced by vibrating objects and propagates as longitudinal waves through a medium.
Speed of sound depends on the medium and its temperature: v = √(B/ρ) for solids and liquids, v in gases increases with temperature.
Frequency determines pitch; higher frequency → higher pitch; amplitude determines loudness.
Ultrasound (>20 kHz) is used in medical imaging, cleaning, and SONAR; Infrasound (<20 Hz) is used to detect earthquakes or volcanic eruptions.
Echo occurs when sound reflects from a surface; Reverberation is multiple reflected sounds causing prolonged effect.
Musical instruments produce sound via vibrating strings, air columns, or membranes; length, tension, and thickness affect frequency.
Sound energy can be absorbed, reflected, refracted, and diffracted by different media.
formulas: Speed of sound: v = d / t
Frequency: f = number of vibrations / time
Fundamental frequency of string: f = (1/2L) √(T/μ), where L = length, T = tension, μ = mass per unit length
Fundamental frequency of air column (open): f = v / 2L; (closed): f = v / 4L
examples: A tuning fork of 512 Hz vibrates; its sound has a pitch of 512 Hz.
Sound travels faster in steel (≈5100 m/s) than in air (≈343 m/s).
If a cliff is 686 m away and echo is heard after 4 s, speed of sound v = 343 m/s.
An open pipe of length 0.85 m produces a fundamental note; frequency f = v / 2L = 343 / 1.7 ≈ 201.76 Hz.
Ultrasound imaging uses sound waves above 20 kHz to produce images of internal organs.
exam_tips: Use formulas carefully, especially for echo and wavelength calculations.
Remember the difference between echo (single reflection) and reverberation (multiple reflections).
Link pitch, frequency, and wavelength in problem-solving.
Revise applications of ultrasound and infrasound for real-life examples.
Be aware of factors affecting speed of sound in different media.
